description: This column is about describing the content type of the HTTP response in the web application or API service. It likely indicates the format or type of data that the response contains, such as JSON, XML, HTML, etc.
This column is about storing the actual content of HTTP responses from the web application or API service.

This column is about storing error messages related to HTTP responses in the context of the web application or API service. It may include details on errors encountered during HTTP communication, such as connection issues, status code errors, or other response-related errors.

This column is about storing unique identifiers for the buckets in the storage management module of the web application or API service. Each bucket will have its own unique ID for identification and management purposes.

This column is about storing the names of buckets within the storage management system. Buckets are units used for storing data in the system. Each bucket likely has a unique name to differentiate it from others and facilitate data retrieval and management.

This column is about specifying the allowed MIME types for files in the storage buckets. MIME types are used to indicate the nature and format of a document or file and are crucial for determining how the file should be handled by the web application or service, such as rendering images, playing audio or video, or displaying text content.

This column is about storing the owner's ID associated with the buckets in the storage management aspect of the web application or API service. It is likely used to track which user or entity owns and has permissions over specific data storage buckets.

This column is about storing the unique identifiers of analytics buckets in the storage system. These identifiers are used to track and manage various analytics data stored in different buckets within the system.
This column is about the type of extensions used for real-time features in the web application or service.
This column is about the authentication flow state related to the authentication code in the context of user authentication and authorization processes in the web application/service.
This column is about managing the code challenge associated with the authentication flow state for the authentication process in the web application or API service.

This column is about storing the provider type associated with the authentication flow state in the context of the web application or API service. It likely categorizes the type of provider being used for authentication purposes, such as OAuth providers, SAML providers, etc.

This column is about the provider access token associated with the authentication flow state in the system. It likely stores the access token provided by the authentication provider for the user's current authentication session.
This column is about storing the refresh token provided by the authentication provider during the authentication flow state.

This column is about the authentication method used in the flow state of the authentication process for this web application or API service. It likely stores information about how users are authenticated, such as through username/password, social login, biometrics, multi-factor authentication, etc.

This column is about storing the names of hooks associated with Supabase functions. Hooks in this context likely refer to event triggers or callbacks that are executed in response to specific actions or changes in the database or application.

This column is about the HTTP methods used in the HTTP request queue of the web application or API service. It may contain values such as GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH, etc., indicating the type of request being made.

This column stores the URLs related to an HTTP request queue in the context of the web application or API service. It likely contains the URLs to which the requests in the queue are directed or from where they originated.
This column is about the names of the storage namespaces in the Iceberg storage system. It likely stores the names of namespaces used for organizing and managing data within the storage system.

This column is about the names of Iceberg tables related to storage management in the project's database. These tables are likely used to store information about data storage, buckets, object storage, and other related aspects of the application's storage infrastructure.

This column is about the location information of the Iceberg tables in the storage. Iceberg tables are related to the storage management of the web application or API service. They may contain details such as where the tables are physically stored, which storage system or service they are using, and other metadata related to the storage of data within the application.
This column is about identifying the provider associated with user identities for authentication purposes.
This column is about the provider of user identities used for authentication in the system.

examples: user_auth_method: username_password, session_timeout: 1800, mfa_enabled: true, oauth_provider: google
user_auth_method: fingerprint_scan, session_timeout: 3600, mfa_enabled: false, oauth_provider: none
user_auth_method: face_recognition, session_timeout: 3000, mfa_enabled: true, oauth_provider: facebook
user_auth_method: pin_code, session_timeout: 2400, mfa_enabled: true, oauth_provider: twitter

user_auth_method: smart_card, session_timeout: 3600, mfa_enabled: false, oauth_provider: none
user_auth_method: voice_recognition, session_timeout: 3000, mfa_enabled: true, oauth_provider: github
user_auth_method: passwordless, session_timeout: 2400, mfa_enabled: true, oauth_provider: slack
user_auth_method: hardware_token, session_timeout: 1800, mfa_enabled: true, oauth_provider: apple
user_auth_method: biometric_verification, session_timeout: 3600, mfa_enabled: false, oauth_provider: none
user_auth_method: security_question, session_timeout: 3000, mfa_enabled: true, oauth_provider: amazon
user_auth_method: social_login, session_timeout: 2400, mfa_enabled: true, oauth_provider: microsoft
user_auth_method: voiceprint, session_timeout: 1800, mfa_enabled: true, oauth_provider: yahoo
user_auth_method: pattern_unlock, session_timeout: 3600, mfa_enabled: false, oauth_provider: none
user_auth_method: fingerprint_and_pin, session_timeout: 3000, mfa_enabled: true, oauth_provider: instagram
user_auth_method: palm_vein_scan, session_timeout: 2400, mfa_enabled: true, oauth_provider: github
user_auth_method: certificate_authentication, session_timeout: 1800, mfa_enabled: true, oauth_provider: salesforce
user_auth_method: voice_biometrics, session_timeout: 3600, mfa_enabled: false, oauth_provider: none
user_auth_method: email_verification, session_timeout: 3000, mfa_enabled: true, oauth_provider: slack
user_auth_method: device_fingerprinting, session_timeout: 2400, mfa_enabled: true, oauth_provider: apple
description: This column is about the raw configuration details related to authentication instances in the project. It likely includes settings and configurations specific to the authentication module or functionality, such as user authentication methods, session management configurations, multi-factor authentication settings, OAuth configurations, secrets management details, and potentially other relevant parameters for handling user authentication and authorization within the web application or API service.
This column is related to managing encryption keys using the pgsodium library. It likely stores the name of the encryption key used for securing sensitive data within the database.

examples: Randomly generated key for token authentication
Key for encrypting credit card information
Secret key for secure user access
Unique identifier key for user profiles
Session key for secure data transfer
Recovery key for accessing locked accounts
description: This column is about storing comments related to keys managed by pgsodium in the database table.
This column is likely related to encryption or security measures for protecting user data in the project. It may store encryption keys or data related to secure handling of user information.

This column is about topics of real-time messages being exchanged in the web application or API service. It likely stores the topics or channels through which real-time messages are sent and received, facilitating communication between different components or users in real-time.

examples: display typing indicator when user is typing
notify user when message is received
reconnect to websocket on network error
broadcast system messages to all users
description: This column is about storing information related to real-time messages and their extensions in the web application or API service project. It likely contains data that helps manage and extend the functionality of real-time messaging features within the system.

examples: Reminder: Task 'Meeting with the team' starts in 30 minutes
Warning: Invalid login attempt from IP 123.45.67.89
Emergency: Application server crashed, investigating root cause
New comment on post 'Product launch announcement' by user 'MiaJones'
Alert: Low disk space on server 'ServerABC'
Notification: Your order #12345 has been shipped
Reminder: Task 'Review quarterly reports' due in 2 days
Error: Failed to process payment for order #54321
Admin alert: Unauthorized access attempt detected
description: This column is about handling real-time messages related to events in the web application or API service. It likely stores data concerning the events triggered in real-time, such as user interactions, system notifications, or any other real-time activities happening within the application.
This column is about multi-factor authentication (MFA) authentication method claims related to user authentication in the system.

This column is about storing the one-time password code used for Multi-Factor Authentication challenges in the authentication process of the web application or API service. It likely holds temporary codes generated for users to verify their identity along with other authentication factors.
This column is about storing the friendly names of multi-factor authentication factors associated with user authentication.
This column is about storing the secret key related to multi-factor authentication factors for authentication purposes.
This column is about storing the phone number associated with multi-factor authentication factors for user authentication.
This column is about storing the names of schema migrations. It likely tracks the names or identifiers of schema migration files or scripts used to update the database schema structure over time.
This column is about storing hashes related to schema migrations in the database. It likely contains data that helps track the versioning or state of schema migrations within the system.

This column 'supabase_functions.migrations.version' likely contains the version information related to schema migrations for Supabase functions. It may store the version number or identifier of the schema migration being executed or the current version of the migrations being applied within the Supabase functions environment.
This column is about the unique identifier for each authorization in the OAuth authorizations table. It likely serves as the primary key for this table.
This column is about storing the redirect URI associated with OAuth authorizations. It likely contains the URI where users will be redirected to after granting authorization to the application.

This column is about storing the scope of the authorization request in the OAuth authorizations table. It likely contains information about the permissions or access levels requested by the client application during the authorization process.
This column is about storing the state associated with an OAuth authorization request in the web application or API service.

This column is about storing the resource associated with an OAuth authorization. It could represent the specific functionality or data that the authorization is granting access to within the web application or service.

This column is about storing the code challenge associated with OAuth authorizations. The code challenge is a unique value generated by the client to verify the authorization request in OAuth flows. Storing this value in the database allows for validation and verification during the authentication process.
This column contains the authorization code generated during the OAuth authorization process for user authentication and authorization.
This column is about storing the hashed client secret used for authenticating OAuth clients within the authentication and authorization functionalities of the web application or API service.

This column stores the redirect URIs associated with the OAuth clients. Redirect URIs are the URLs to which the OAuth server will redirect the user after they have granted permission to the client application.

This column is about the grant types associated with OAuth clients. It likely contains data specifying the types of grants or permissions that the OAuth clients are authorized to use, such as client credentials, authorization code, implicit, resource owner password credentials, or refresh token.

This column is about storing the names of OAuth clients registered in the authentication system of the web application or API service. Each entry in this column represents the client name associated with a particular OAuth client.

This column is about storing the URI of the client in the OAuth client table associated with the web application or API service. It likely holds the web address or endpoint where the client application can be accessed.

This column stores the URI of the logo associated with the OAuth client in the authentication system. It provides a link to the image representing the client in the user interface or any related branding purposes.

This column is about the scopes associated with OAuth consents in the authentication process of the web application or API service. These scopes may define the level of access or permissions granted to the user by the OAuth consent, such as read, write, delete access to certain resources or functionalities.

This column is about storing the names of objects within the storage management system. It likely holds the names or identifiers of files, documents, or data stored within the application's storage system.

This column is about storing the version information of objects in the storage system. It likely tracks different versions of objects as they are created or updated within the storage management component of the web application or API service.

This column is about storing the unique identifier of the owner of the objects in the storage. It may reference another table that holds information about the owners, such as user details or permissions.
This column is about storing the hashed values of one-time tokens related to authentication.
This column is about relating one-time tokens to something else within the authentication system.

This column is about storing prefixes associated with names in the context of managing data storage in the application. It may involve defining naming conventions, categorizing data based on prefixes, or organizing data in storage buckets using specific prefixes.

This column is about storing the file path or URL location of logos associated with QR codes. It is used to link the QR codes to the specific logo images that may be displayed when the QR codes are scanned or used in the web application or service.

This column is about storing refresh tokens used for authentication. Refresh tokens are tokens that can be used to obtain new access tokens without needing the user to re-authenticate. They are typically issued to a client during the initial authentication process and are used to maintain a user's session and access levels.

This column is about storing the user ID associated with the refresh tokens used for authentication purposes in the authentication module of the web application or API service. Refresh tokens are typically used to obtain new access tokens without requiring the user to log in again.

This column is about the parent relationship in the 'refresh_tokens' table in the authentication system. It likely stores the relationship or reference to the parent record in the refresh_tokens table, which could be used for managing token refreshes and their corresponding user sessions.
This column is about storing the unique identifier for S3 multipart uploads in the context of the web application or service's storage management functionality.

This column is about storing the unique signature for the upload process related to S3 multipart uploads in the storage module. The signature is likely used to authenticate and authorize the upload request for multipart files on the S3 platform.
This column is about storing keys related to S3 multipart uploads specifically within the storage aspect of the project.

This column is about storing the version information related to S3 multipart uploads in the database table. It likely tracks the version or revision of the S3 multipart upload being managed by the web application or service.
This column is about storing the owner ID linked to S3 multipart uploads in the application's storage system.
This column is about storing keys for parts of a multipart upload process in an S3 bucket. Each part of the upload process is identified by a key in this column.

This column is about storing the ETag (entity tag) values associated with the parts of multipart uploads in an Amazon S3 (Simple Storage Service) bucket. ETags are used for identifying different parts of a file being uploaded in multiple parts to S3, allowing for efficient and reliable handling of large file uploads.
This column is about storing the owner ID associated with the parts of S3 multipart uploads in the database table.
This column is about tracking the version of parts uploaded as part of an S3 multipart upload process in the storage of the application or service.

This column is about storing the entity IDs for the SAML providers used for authentication in the web application or service. These entity IDs uniquely identify each SAML provider and are likely used for configuring and managing authentication settings and interactions with these providers.

This column is about storing metadata XML related to SAML providers for authentication within the web application or API service. SAML (Security Assertion Markup Language) is used for exchanging authentication and authorization data between parties, and the metadata XML likely contains information about the SAML provider configuration, endpoints, certificates, and other relevant details for enabling secure SAML-based authentication.
This column is about storing the metadata URL for SAML providers used in authentication.

This column is about the name ID format used by SAML providers for authentication in the context of the web application or API service. It likely stores information related to how user identities are represented and managed within the SAML authentication process.
This column is about managing SAML relay states for authentication in the context of the project's authentication and authorization functionalities.
This column is about storing the SAML relay state associated with the email address of the user during the authentication process for SAML providers.

This column is about storing the redirect destinations for SAML relay states used in the authentication flow of the web application or API service. It likely contains URLs or paths to which the user should be redirected after completing the SAML authentication process.
This column is about tracking the version of schema migrations related to authentication in the database schema.

This column is about storing the versions of schema migrations in the Supabase database. It likely records the version number of each schema migration applied to the database to track and manage changes to the database structure over time.

examples: CREATE TABLE users (id serial PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(50), email VARCHAR(100));
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN date_of_birth DATE;
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN country VARCHAR(50);
CREATE TABLE posts (id serial PRIMARY KEY, user_id INTEGER REFERENCES users(id));
ALTER TABLE users RENAME COLUMN name TO full_name;
ALTER TABLE posts DROP COLUMN user_id;
CREATE ROLE customer WITH LOGIN PASSWORD 'customerpass';
GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA public TO customer;
REVOKE INSERT ON users FROM customer;
CREATE VIEW active_users AS SELECT * FROM users WHERE is_active = true;
ALTER TABLE users ADD CONSTRAINT unique_email UNIQUE (email);
ALTER TABLE posts ADD CONSTRAINT fk_user_id FOREIGN KEY (user_id) REFERENCES users(id);
ALTER TABLE users SET DEFAULT 'Unknown' FOR country;
CREATE TRIGGER audit_trigger BEFORE INSERT OR UPDATE ON users FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION audit_function();
ALTER SEQUENCE users_id_seq OWNED BY users.id;
description: This column is about the SQL statements executed during schema migrations in the Supabase framework.

This column is about storing the names of schema migrations related to the Supabase project. It likely contains identifiers or names for the schema migrations that are used for managing database schema changes or updates within the Supabase project.

This column is about storing the names of secrets in a vault. It likely contains unique identifiers or names assigned to different secrets stored in a secure vault within the web application or service. These secrets could include API keys, passwords, tokens, or any sensitive information required for the application's functioning.

This column is about storing secret information in a vault within the database. It likely contains sensitive data that needs to be securely stored, such as API keys, passwords, encryption keys, or other confidential information required for the application's functionalities like encryption, authentication, or authorization.

description: This column is about storing the user agents associated with authentication sessions in the system. It likely captures the details of the user agents or client devices used when sessions are established for user authentication.
This column is about tagging sessions related to user authentication in the system.

This column is about storing the domain names related to single sign-on (SSO) authentication for users. It likely contains the domain names of identity providers used for SSO functionality in the web application or API service.

This column is about storing the unique identifier (resource_id) of Single Sign-On (SSO) providers associated with user authentication and authorization. It likely links to a table or entity that manages configurations and information related to SSO providers, such as their names, settings, and integration details within the larger web application or API service.

This column is likely related to the real-time functionality of the application or service, specifically to identify the name of the tenants interacting within the real-time environment. It could be used to track and manage different tenants or clients accessing the real-time features of the application.
This column is about the external identifier for tenants in the real-time component of the system.

This column is related to storing JWT secrets specifically for tenants in the real-time aspect of the application. It is likely used for authenticating and authorizing tenants within the real-time features of the web application or API service.
This column is about user identification within the authentication system. It likely stores a unique identifier for each user in the system.

This column is about the role of users in the authentication system. It likely stores information about the level of access or permissions each user has within the system, such as admin, moderator, regular user, etc.
This column is about storing the email addresses of users in the authentication system.
This column is about storing the encrypted passwords of users in the authentication system. It likely contains hashed and salted passwords to ensure the security of user accounts.
This column is used to store the confirmation token generated for user authentication and verification purposes in the 'auth.users' table.
This column is used to store the recovery token associated with a user for password recovery purposes.
This column is about storing the newly generated token that facilitates changing the email address associated with a user account in the authentication system.

This column is about storing the email change token associated with a user in the authentication system. It likely facilitates the process of changing a user's email address by generating and storing a token that can be used to verify and authorize the email change request.

This column is about storing the IP addresses of visitors who have accessed the web application or API service. It may be used for tracking user locations, monitoring traffic patterns, implementing security measures like rate limiting or blocking malicious users, and performing analytics or logging purposes.

description: This column is about storing user agent information for each visit to the web application or API service. The user agent typically includes details about the browser, device, and operating system used by the visitor.
